title: Function Arguments and Return Values
description: Understand arguments, default values, and how to return results.

slides.title: Arguments with Default Values
slides.content:
You can give arguments default values, making them optional when calling.

```r
power <- function(x, exponent = 2) {
  x^exponent
}
```

Now `power(3)` uses exponent=2, `power(3, 3)` uses exponent=3.

slides.title: Default Arguments

quizData.question: Given `f <- function(a = 1, b = 2) a + b`, what does `f(5)` return?
quizData.explanation: a is set to 5, b uses default 2, so 5+2=7.

slides.title: Returning Multiple Values
Multiple Return
Argument Matching
slides.content:
R matches arguments in three ways:
1. By position: `f(3,5)`
2. By name: `f(a=3, b=5)`
3. Partial matching (not recommended)

You can mix positional and named, but named arguments are often clearer.
